questions.text: What is the primary advantage of Vite over Create React App for development?
options: Vite supports more JavaScript frameworks
Vite serves source files as native ES modules, making dev server startup nearly instant
Vite produces smaller production bundles
Vite doesn't require Node.js
questions.correct: Vite serves source files as native ES modules, making dev server startup nearly instant
questions.explanation: Vite's key innovation is using native ES modules during development. Instead of bundling the entire app before serving (like Webpack/CRA), Vite transforms files on-demand as the browser requests them, resulting in sub-second server starts regardless of app size.

questions.text: Why should you avoid using array indices as keys when rendering lists in React?
options: Array indices are not unique
React doesn't accept numbers as keys
Index-based keys cause incorrect component reuse when items are reordered, added, or removed
Using indices is slower than string keys
questions.correct: Index-based keys cause incorrect component reuse when items are reordered, added, or removed
questions.explanation: React uses keys to identify which items changed. If you use indices and insert an item at the beginning, every subsequent item's key changes, causing React to destroy and recreate all those components instead of just adding one. This leads to bugs with component state and unnecessary DOM operations.

questions.text: What happens if you provide no dependency array to useEffect?
options: The effect never runs
The effect runs only on mount
The effect runs after every render
The effect runs once and then is garbage collected
questions.correct: The effect runs after every render
questions.explanation: With no dependency array, useEffect runs after every single render (including re-renders). With an empty array [], it runs once after initial render. With [a, b], it runs when a or b changes. Omitting the array is usually a bug that can cause performance issues or infinite loops.

questions.text: When should you use the functional updater form of useState (e.g., setCount(prev => prev + 1))?
options: Always — it's the recommended form for all state updates
When the new state depends on the previous state value
Only when using useState inside useEffect
Only for number state variables
questions.correct: When the new state depends on the previous state value
questions.explanation: The functional updater ensures you're working with the latest state value. This matters when multiple updates happen in quick succession (like rapid clicks) or when the update is inside a stale closure (like a setTimeout). Direct updates like setCount(count + 1) may use a stale 'count' value.

questions.text: What is a custom hook in React?
options: A class component that extends React.Hook
A special built-in React function for advanced state management
A regular JavaScript function that starts with 'use' and calls other hooks to encapsulate reusable stateful logic
A third-party library for adding new hooks to React
questions.correct: A regular JavaScript function that starts with 'use' and calls other hooks to encapsulate reusable stateful logic
questions.explanation: Custom hooks are just functions whose names start with 'use'. They can call other hooks (useState, useEffect, etc.) and return whatever values are useful. They're the primary mechanism for extracting and sharing stateful logic between components without changing the component hierarchy.
